title: Speak But the Word
subtitle: The centurion of Capernaum pleads for his paralysed servant, and Christ marvels at his faith

stillness: Be still, and let the noise of the world fall away.
He came to the Lord by faith, desire, and devotion, rather than by the step of bodily presence.
Draw near as he did — not with your feet, but with your heart.
gospel: Jesus began to draw near to the city of Capernaum.
A centurion, captain of a hundred soldiers, came to him.
He sent the elders of the Jews, pleading and saying:
"Lord, my servant lies paralysed in the house, and is grievously tormented."
Jesus answered: "I will come and heal him." And he went with them.
And when he was now not far from the house, the centurion sent friends, saying:
"Lord, do not trouble yourself, for I am not worthy that you should enter under my roof.
Therefore neither did I think myself worthy to come to you;
but only say the word, and my servant shall be healed.
For I also am a man set under authority, having soldiers under me;
and I say to one, Go, and he goes; and to another, Come, and he comes;
and to my servant, Do this, and he does it."
Hearing this, Jesus marvelled, and said: "I have not found so great a faith in Israel."
behold: Behold this centurion — a Gentile, a stranger by birth, but a kinsman in heart.
A stranger by nation, yet near by faith; a captain of soldiers, yet a companion of the Angels.
He did not dare to come to Jesus in his own body, judging himself unworthy of Christ's presence.
See how he calls his slave "my boy" — out of tenderness, not contempt, against the proud who scorn their servants.
Behold his three words — he lies, he is paralysed, he is tormented — spoken to bend the Lord to mercy.
He laid bare only the sickness, and left the remedy to the power of Christ's mercy.
He does not say "palace," nor even "house," though he was a prince — but only "roof," out of humility.
Behold the Lord coming: "I will come" — there is his lowliness; "and heal him" — there is his tenderness.
consider: In this captain three wondrous virtues are shown to us: humility, faith, and prudence.
Great humility — for when the Lord stood ready to come, he judged himself unworthy to receive him under his roof.
Perfect faith — for, a Gentile, he believed that God could give health to the boy by a word alone.
No small prudence — for beyond the flesh he knew the hidden Godhead, and understood that the One he saw walking bodily was everywhere present through divinity.
Nor was charity wanting in him: while many came to plead for their own and their children, he pleaded only for a servant's healing.
Learn from him to have compassion on your servants and your subjects, and to bear care for them.
He was not like many today, who, seeing their subjects fall sick, neglect them.
By calling himself unworthy, he showed himself worthy — not one within whose walls, but into whose heart Christ would enter.
Because he made himself unworthy to receive Christ in his house, he was made worthy of the kingdom.
And the Lord marvelled — not that anything could astonish him who works all wonders, but to teach us to marvel and to praise the great works of God.
prayer: Lord Jesus Christ, I am not worthy that you should enter under the roof of my flesh,
for the boy of my sensuality lies paralysed, undone by the sickness of sin in the house of my body,
and is grievously tormented by the stirrings of its desires.
But come by the inpouring of your grace, and speak the word of truth,
that my boy may be healed, and rise again from his sins.
Grant, merciful God, to me — a wretch set under the power of your grace,
and holding from your grace the natural strengths and powers under me —
that I may have the power to drive off harmful thoughts and motions,
to abide in what is good, and to command my body to serve the spirit. Amen.

latinJewel.latin: Domine Jesu Christe, non sum dignus ut intres sub tectum carnis meæ, quia puer sensualitatis jacet paralyticus, dissolutus morbo peccati in domo corporis mei.
latinJewel.english: Lord Jesus Christ, I am not worthy that you should enter under the roof of my flesh, for the boy of my sensuality lies paralysed, undone by the sickness of sin in the house of my body.
